findings.description: Session tokens are generated with the non-cryptographic random module (Mersenne Twister), making tokens predictable and allowing session hijacking; use secrets.token_urlsafe instead.
touch() does not verify the session is unexpired before sliding expiry, so an expired-but-not-yet-purged session can be resurrected indefinitely.
user_sessions() does not purge or filter expired sessions, returning stale entries inconsistently with get() and active_count().
The store has no locking, so concurrent access (e.g. multi-threaded server) can corrupt _sessions during _purge_expired iteration/deletion or lose writes.
get() docstring/class implies sliding renewal but get() never slides expiry, so callers relying on read-to-renew semantics will see premature expiry.
touch() and get() read _sessions without constant-time token comparison; dict lookup timing could theoretically leak token validity, though risk is marginal.
_purge_expired runs a full O(n) scan and rebuilds a dead-key list on every get()/active_count() call, which is wasteful for large stores under high read volume.
destroy() silently ignores unknown tokens and returns None, giving callers no signal of whether a session existed, unlike touch() which returns bool.
Session is a mutable dataclass exposing expires_at and token as plain writable fields, allowing accidental external mutation of stored session state.
